informal_statement.text: Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.
preconditions: the input tree is a valid BST
both query keys are present in the tree
required_properties: the returned key appears in the tree
both query keys are in the subtree rooted at the returned key
no proper descendant of the returned key has both query keys in its subtree
edge_cases: both keys equal (returned key equals them)
one key is an ancestor of the other
keys on opposite sides of the root
tree with only a single path

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST.
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree.
common_failure_modes: returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key.
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys.
common_failure_modes: using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem inorder_strictly_ascending_of_bst {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) (hin : InorderStrict t) : Inorder t = sort (Inorder t) ∧ InorderStrict t
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Assume the input tree is a BST and its in-order traversal is strictly ascending; this provides the ordering property needed for correctness.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem keys_present {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : ∃ n : Node α, Mem k1 (subtree n) ∧ Mem k2 (subtree n)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Since both keys are present in the tree, there exists at least one node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def lowest_both (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) : Prop := ∃ n, Mem k1 (subtree n) ∧ Mem k2 (subtree n) ∧ ∀ m, (m ≠ n → ¬ (Mem k1 (subtree m) ∧ Mem k2 (subtree m))) ∧ (n is ancestor of all nodes with both keys)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the target property: the 'lowest' node is the one that contains both keys and is below (i.e., is not above) any other node that also contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem lowest_both_unique {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : ∃! n, Mem k1 (subtree n) ∧ Mem k2 (subtree n) ∧ LowestNode n t k1 k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Under BST + strict in-order (no duplicates), the lowest node containing both keys is unique.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def answer_key (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) : α := (lowest_node t k1 k2).key
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The desired output is the key stored at the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_returns_some_key {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : ∃ r, rust_impl t k1 k2 = some r
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Show the reference implementation returns a key (not None / error) when both keys are present.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_invariant_subtree_contains {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : let r := rust_impl t k1 k2; ∀ step, current_node step has both keys in its subtree
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove the loop/recursion invariant: at each step, the current candidate node’s subtree still contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_progress_towards_lowest {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : rust_impl t k1 k2 terminates ∧ candidate_node moves strictly downward when possible
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove termination and that the algorithm moves downward toward the lowest node that can still contain both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_result_contains_both {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : let r := rust_impl t k1 k2; Mem k1 t_subtree_of_result r ∧ Mem k2 t_subtree_of_result r
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned key corresponds to a node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_result_is_lowest {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : let r := rust_impl t k1 k2; LowestNode (node_with_key r) t k1 k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned node is the lowest node (no descendant below it contains both keys).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_matches_spec {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} (hk1 : Mem k1 t) (hk2 : Mem k2 t) : rust_impl t k1 k2 = answer_key t k1 k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Overall correctness: the implementation’s returned key equals the key of the lowest node containing both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_respects_bst_ordering {t : Tree α} (hbst : BST t) {k1 k2 : α} : decisions_taken_by_impl_follow_inorder_order t k1 k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Justify that the branching decisions made by the algorithm are consistent with BST ordering (e.g., comparisons route the search correctly).
